skill.name: typescript-pro
skill.description: Master TypeScript with advanced types, generics, and strict type safety. Handles complex type systems, decorators, and enterprise-grade patterns.
skill.summary: Advanced TypeScript expert for enterprise-grade type systems and architecture
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Prompt-only skill with no executable code. No security risks detected. The skill provides TypeScript development guidance without accessing filesystem, network, environment variables, or executing external commands.

content.user_title: Master Advanced TypeScript Architecture and Type Safety
content.value_statement: Struggling with complex TypeScript types and generics in production systems. This skill provides expert guidance on enterprise-grade TypeScript architecture, advanced type systems, and strict type safety patterns.
actual_capabilities: Design advanced TypeScript type systems with generics and conditional types
Configure strict TypeScript compiler options for production safety
Create reusable utility types and type declarations
Optimize type inference and build performance
Implement decorators and metadata programming patterns
Architect type-safe integration with React, Node.js, and Express
limitations: Cannot execute or compile TypeScript code directly
Does not install dependencies or modify build configurations
Cannot verify runtime behavior of type assertions
Limited to TypeScript-specific guidance (not general JavaScript advice)

use_cases.title: Enterprise Type System Design
use_cases.description: Senior architects designing shared type libraries and contracts for large codebases with multiple teams.

use_cases.title: Complex Generic Solutions
use_cases.description: Developers solving advanced type challenges involving conditional types, mapped types, and type inference edge cases.

use_cases.title: Production Type Safety Hardening
use_cases.description: Teams migrating codebases to strict TypeScript and eliminating any type violations for production systems.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Type Safety Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure strict TypeScript for my new project with proper compiler flags and linting rules.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new TypeScript project with enterprise-grade type safety

prompt_templates.title: Generic Type Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a generic function that handles [specific use case] with proper type constraints and inference.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating reusable generic utilities for your codebase

prompt_templates.title: Complex Type Inference
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a conditional type that [describes complex type transformation] while maintaining type safety.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my TypeScript architecture for potential type safety issues and suggest improvements for strict mode compliance.
prompt_templates.scenario: Auditing existing codebase for type safety improvements

output_examples.input: Design a type-safe API client with generic response handling
output: Defines generic ApiResponse<T> interface with success/error discrimination
Implements typed fetch wrapper with request/response type validation
Creates utility types for endpoint definitions and error types
Provides example usage with proper type inference and error handling

output_examples.input: Create a deep readonly utility type for nested objects
output: Handles arrays, objects, and primitive types correctly
Includes example usage and test cases
Explains type distribution and inference behavior

best_practices: Always enable strict mode and noImplicitAny in tsconfig.json for new projects
Prefer type inference over explicit annotations when the compiler can infer types correctly
Use branded types and type guards for runtime validation of data from external sources
Leverage utility types (Partial, Required, Readonly, Pick, Omit) instead of custom implementations
anti_patterns: Using the 'any' type to bypass type errors instead of properly modeling types
Suppressing TypeScript errors with @ts-ignore without understanding the root cause
Duplicating type definitions instead of using utility types or proper generics
Over-annotating code with types that the compiler can infer automatically

faq.question: When should I use this skill instead of general coding assistance?
faq.answer: Use this skill for TypeScript-specific challenges like advanced generics, type inference issues, or strict mode configuration. Use general coding help for JavaScript, runtime logic, or non-TypeScript concerns.

faq.question: Can this skill write TypeScript code for me?
faq.answer: This skill provides TypeScript patterns, type definitions, and architectural guidance. It cannot directly write or execute files, but generates code you can implement in your project.

faq.question: Does this skill support TypeScript 5.x features?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill supports modern TypeScript including decorators (stage 3), conditional types, mapped types, and other advanced features from recent TypeScript versions.

faq.question: Can this skill help migrate JavaScript to TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill provides migration strategies, type modeling approaches, and gradual typing patterns for converting JavaScript codebases to TypeScript.

faq.question: How does this skill handle framework-specific TypeScript?
faq.answer: This skill covers TypeScript patterns for React, Node.js, Express, and other frameworks. Focus on type modeling rather than framework-specific implementation details.

faq.question: What strictness level should I use for my project?
faq.answer: For new projects, use strict mode with noImplicitAny and strictNullChecks. For migrations, start gradual and increase strictness incrementally. This skill helps configure both approaches.
